title: No silent false-pass; robust file and error handling
description: WHAT: MCP check_workspace returns passed true for a non-existent or empty path (mcp_server.py resolves and rglobs silently) which is a false success on the agent-facing surface; the CLI prints raw tracebacks for a missing or malformed profile or workspace; the checker reads files with unguarded read_text() and crashes on non-UTF-8 or unreadable files. WHERE: src/ob/mcp_server.py, src/ob/cli.py, src/ob/commands/check.py, src/ob/profile.py. HOW: validate existence in the MCP report and return an explicit error or passed false rather than passed true; wrap CLI command bodies to print a friendly one-line error and exit non-zero on FileNotFoundError and ValidationError; read files with error-tolerant decoding and skip plus note unreadable files. Non-goals: not changing the violation schema. Flagged by code-reviewer C1.
acceptance_criteria: MCP check_workspace on a non-existent path never returns passed true (returns passed false or an explicit error). ob check on a missing path and on a malformed profile print a clear one-line error and exit 1 with no traceback. A non-UTF-8 file in a workspace does not crash the checker.
close_reason: AC1: MCP check_workspace on a non-existent path returns passed=false + error (test_tool_nonexistent_path_is_not_silent_pass) — added is_dir() guard in check_workspace_report. AC2: ob check on missing workspace/profile and schema-invalid profile print 'Error: ...' and exit 1 with no uncaught exception (test_check_missing_workspace_friendly/missing_profile_friendly/malformed_profile_friendly) via _exit_error/_load_profile_or_exit/_validate_workspace_or_exit in cli.py. AC3: non-UTF-8 file skipped not fatal (test_non_utf8_file_does_not_crash) via _safe_read. 93 tests, ruff clean.

title: Close convention-checker detection gaps
description: WHAT: the checker misses real violations. _check_forbidden_imports only inspects ast.ImportFrom so import skimage._shared is never flagged, and it matches import paths against filesystem forbidden_paths so src-layout profiles (diffusers src/diffusers/_internal/) never fire the forbidden-import rule. _check_deprecated_apis only matches from X import Y and misses idiomatic ski.filters.median(...) attribute usage and multiline imports. _check_docstring_style only handles numpydoc and never enforces required_sections for any style. WHERE: src/ob/commands/check.py, src/ob/models.py, profiles/*.yaml. HOW: handle both ast.Import and ast.ImportFrom; normalize an import-root or forbidden-import prefix so flat and src layouts both match; detect a deprecated symbol via attribute access on the imported alias and across multiline imports; enforce required_sections presence generically and warn on unsupported docstring styles. Non-goals: no full semantic analysis. Flagged by code-reviewer C3/C4 and architect I1/I2.
acceptance_criteria: import skimage._shared.utils is flagged SK-F-001. A deprecated symbol used as ski.filters.median(...) is flagged SK-D-001. Under the diffusers profile a forbidden _internal import fires DIFF-F-001. A numpydoc function missing a required section is flagged. Documented example counts are re-locked in the final child.
close_reason: Rewrote checks AST-first. AC1: plain 'import skimage._shared.utils' flagged SK-F-001 (test_detects_plain_forbidden_import) — _imported_modules now covers ast.Import+ImportFrom. AC2: attribute usage ski.filters.median(...) flagged SK-D-001 (test_detects_deprecated_attribute_usage) via import-binding resolution. AC3: src-layout forbidden import fires DIFF-F-001 (test_diffusers_forbidden_import_fires_with_src_layout) via new profile.import_root='src/' stripping. AC4: numpydoc fn missing Examples flagged (test_flags_missing_required_docstring_section) — docstring check now enforces required_sections for any style, one DOC-001/fn. Counts re-locked: test_bad_contrib_count_locked==5 SK, test_diffusers_bad_contrib_count_locked==2 DIFF. safe example still passes. 88 tests, ruff clean.

title: Scaffold output and demo.sh cold-path are clean
description: WHAT: ob scaffold emits stubs with an unused numpy import (scaffold.py source and test stubs) so the documented ruff check . fails on generated code in workspaces/ (which ruff does not exclude); scaffold also refuses any task whose text merely contains a forbidden-dir substring like build or shared and then prints a wrong forbidden-target reason (scaffold.py forbidden-name loop); empty or digit-only tasks slugify to invalid module names; demo.sh never activates .venv and set -euo pipefail aborts the script on the by-design non-zero exit of the first ob check. WHERE: src/ob/commands/scaffold.py, src/ob/guardrails.py, demo.sh, pyproject.toml ruff config. HOW: make stubs lint-clean (use np in the source stub or drop the import; drop numpy from the test stub); base the forbidden refusal on the resolved target directory not a raw task substring, and make the message accurate; sanitize the inferred module name; activate the venv in demo.sh and stop treating expected non-zero exits as fatal. Non-goals: not redesigning the scaffold templates wholesale. Flagged by all four compatibility reviewers (scaffold lint was the single most-cited issue) and code-reviewer C2.
acceptance_criteria: A freshly scaffolded workspace passes ruff check . with zero errors. A task whose text contains build or shared but targets an approved directory is NOT refused. The refusal message appears only for genuinely forbidden targets and names the real reason. bash demo.sh runs end to end from a fresh shell with no command-not-found and no early abort.
close_reason: AC1: fresh scaffold passes ruff (ruff check . clean incl workspaces/; test_stubs_pass_ruff_check). Removed unused numpy from source+test stubs. AC2: tasks containing build/shared no longer falsely refused (test_does_not_falsely_refuse_build_word/shared_word) — refusal now matches underscored forbidden leaf or full path via _references_forbidden_path. AC3: genuine forbidden targets still refused (test_refuses_forbidden_path/shared_path; demo.sh Section 4 shows refusal). AC4: bash demo.sh runs end-to-end to 'All sections demonstrated successfully' (activates .venv, dropped set -e so by-design ob-check exit 1 no longer aborts). Slug/module edge cases handled (test_numeric/symbol_task). 82 tests pass.

title: Verify and harden the live Cursor-rule 'catch' moment (centerpiece)
description:
WHAT: The demo's highest-impact beat (docs/demo.md Section 1, the "strong yes" moment) is a Cursor rule catching a convention violation live in the editor when the engineer asks the agent to review examples/bad-first-contrib. The rule files exist (.cursor/rules/scikit-image-conventions.mdc + 4 SDLC-stage rules) but their live activation has never been verified, and rule firing depends on correct frontmatter (description/globs/alwaysApply) and clear, imperative guidance.

WHERE: .cursor/rules/scikit-image-conventions.mdc (and siblings), examples/bad-first-contrib/, docs/demo.md, new docs/cursor-rule-verification.md.

HOW:
- Audit every rule's frontmatter: confirm `globs` actually match the files used in the demo (e.g. scikit-image-conventions globs **/*.py must match examples/bad-first-contrib/filters/_local_contrast.py), and confirm description/alwaysApply are set so the rule is attachable in context.
- Tighten the conventions rule wording so the agent reliably cites the specific violations present in the bad example (deprecated median import, forbidden _shared import, TODO-only body, missing test, Google-style docstring).
- Write docs/cursor-rule-verification.md: a precise, repeatable protocol the presenter runs in Cursor (exact file to open, exact prompt to type, the expected agent response citing rule + each violation), plus the deterministic fallback (`ob check examples/bad-first-contrib`) to run if the model response underwhelms live.

WHY: This is the moment that separates "nice tool" from "strong hire." It must be rehearsed and de-risked, with a graceful fallback, because LLM responses are non-deterministic and this runs live in front of interviewers.

VERIFICATION BOUNDARY: live agent behavior cannot be asserted deterministically from the CLI. The closeable ACs below are the verifiable artifacts (frontmatter validity, glob match, protocol doc, fallback). The actual live firing is a presenter rehearsal step recorded as a note; if it cannot be confirmed in-session, leave this bead in_progress rather than closing on an IOU.

Non-goals: not guaranteeing specific model output; not adding new rules unrelated to the catch moment.
acceptance_criteria:
- Every .cursor/rules/*.mdc file has valid frontmatter (parseable description/globs/alwaysApply).
- The conventions rule's glob demonstrably matches examples/bad-first-contrib/filters/_local_contrast.py (shown by the matching pattern + path).
- docs/cursor-rule-verification.md exists with: exact file to open, exact prompt to use, and the expected agent behavior enumerating each of the 5 seeded violations.
- The verification doc names the deterministic fallback command and where it appears in the demo flow.
- A presenter rehearsal note records the result of running the protocol live in Cursor (pass, or specific gap to fix). [Manual step — not an IOU; recorded as evidence or bead stays in_progress.]
notes:
PROGRESS: Artifacts done + verified.
1. All 5 .cursor/rules/*.mdc have valid frontmatter (checked).
2. scikit-image-conventions glob **/*.py matches examples/bad-first-contrib/filters/_local_contrast.py.
3. Hardened the conventions rule with a "When asked to review a file" section citing the SAME SK-* IDs the CLI emits (SK-D-001/F-001/I-001/T-002/DOC-001).
4. Created docs/cursor-rule-verification.md: exact file, exact prompt, expected 5 violations table, pass criteria, deterministic fallback narration.
5. BONUS fix: render_check_result no longer collapses the Rule-ID column at 80 cols (Rule/Severity fixed+no_wrap, File/Message fold), and all violation file paths are now relative+consistent (was mixing absolute and relative). Reconciled demo.md Section 1 from "four"->"five" violations and linked the protocol doc.

REMAINING (manual, user-only): run the protocol live in Cursor once and confirm the agent cites the rule + violations. Bead stays in_progress until that rehearsal result is recorded (no IOU close).
Code-side blockers resolved by epic xm1: the scikit-image Cursor rule is now generated from the profile (xm1.6) and emits the same five SK-* IDs the CLI does; demo.md's catch-moment beat is self-contained (xm1.9). Remaining: manual rehearsal of the live rule firing in the Cursor UI.

title: CI runs green on GitHub (Actions enabled + MCP deps installed)
description:
WHAT: The CI pipeline is both absent and broken. (a) GitHub Actions REST endpoints return 404 for this repo, indicating Actions is disabled or no run has ever executed. (b) The `test` job in .github/workflows/ci.yml runs `uv pip install -e ".[dev]"` then `pytest`, but `fastmcp` is declared in the `[mcp]` optional-dependency group, not `[dev]`. Therefore tests/test_mcp_server.py raises ImportError at collection time and the job fails. (c) requires-python is ">=3.12" but development happened on 3.14 and CI pins no version, risking drift.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Run `gh run list` (or open the repo Actions tab) -> 404 / no runs exist.
2. In a clean environment: `uv venv && source .venv/bin/activate && uv pip install -e ".[dev]" && pytest` -> collection error: `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'fastmcp'` from tests/test_mcp_server.py (because fastmcp is in [mcp], not [dev]).
3. Note CI pins no Python version while requires-python is >=3.12.

WHERE: pyproject.toml (optional-dependencies), .github/workflows/ci.yml (all jobs that run pytest or ob), GitHub repo Settings > Actions.

HOW:
- Ensure the test job (and any job importing ob.mcp_server) installs fastmcp — either move fastmcp into [dev], or change CI installs to ".[dev,mcp]". Prefer the option that keeps `pytest` green locally and in CI without manual extras.
- Pin a Python version in CI (e.g. 3.12) consistent with requires-python so behavior is reproducible.
- Confirm GitHub Actions is enabled for the repo; if disabled, enable it. Push a commit to trigger a run.
- Watch the run with `gh run watch` / `gh run list` and confirm conclusion=success for every job.

WHY: demo.md Section 6 (line 242) narrates "Show a green CI run on GitHub (pre-pushed)." A red or missing pipeline directly contradicts the demo and the multi-audience claim that briefs appear in the Actions step summary.

Non-goals: not adding new CI jobs beyond what the demo references; not configuring branch protection or required checks; not adding a build matrix.
acceptance_criteria:
- `gh run list` shows at least one completed workflow run on `main` with conclusion success.
- Every job (lint, test, check-bad, check-good, briefs) concludes success.
- The `test` job executes tests/test_mcp_server.py (not skipped) and they pass — proving fastmcp is installed in CI.
- The CI Python version is pinned and satisfies requires-python (>=3.12).
- The `briefs` job's PM/QA/DevOps briefs are visible in the run's step summary.
- `pytest` still passes locally with the documented install command.
notes:
REFINEMENT (plan review): Decision — move fastmcp from [mcp] into [dev] so a single install group (.[dev]) covers local dev, CI, AND fresh-clone MCP loading. Simplest, least surprising, and makes the README quickstart sufficient for the MCP demo too.
USER DEPENDENCY: the 404 on Actions endpoints suggests Actions may be disabled at the repo level. If gh/API cannot enable it, the repo owner must toggle GitHub repo Settings > Actions > "Allow all actions" once. Surface this immediately if hit, rather than silently failing.
PROGRESS (commit 1fc19c7): Code/config DONE and locally verified on Python 3.12 — fastmcp now in dev via self-referential extra; all 5 CI jobs pinned to 3.12; 61 tests + ruff check + ruff format + ob check (bad fails / good passes) all green; uv.lock committed; pushed to main.

BLOCKER (user action needed): CI cannot be verified via gh. Diagnosis: `gh api user` works (kevglynn) but `gh api repos/kevglynn/cursor-takehome-assignment` returns 404 — the active gh token is a fine-grained PAT WITHOUT REST access to this repo. git push works only because it uses SSH. So Actions may already be running; it is unobservable from the CLI.

TO UNBLOCK (either path):
1) Fastest: open https://github.com/kevglynn/cursor-takehome-assignment/actions in a browser, confirm the "CI" workflow ran green for commit 1fc19c7. If Actions is disabled, enable it (Settings > Actions > General > Allow all actions), then re-run.
2) To let the agent verify programmatically: grant the fine-grained PAT Repository access to this repo with Actions:read + Contents:read, OR run `gh auth login`/`gh auth refresh` with a classic token having repo+workflow scopes. Then `gh run list` will work.

Bead stays in_progress until a green run is confirmed (no IOU close).
close_reason: USER-CONFIRMED via gh CLI. The token now has repo+workflow scopes and reads this private repo's Actions; the earlier 404 was a stale session, not a permanent block. Evidence — run 28242226261 on main, conclusion=success: AC1 gh run list shows 3 recent main runs all 'success'. AC2 all 5 jobs success (Lint & Format; Test; ob check bad-first-contrib expect-fail; ob check safe-first-contrib expect-pass; Role Briefs -> Step Summary). AC3 Test job executed tests/test_mcp_server.py (TestMCPResources/ResourceConsistency/ServerRegistration/CheckTool) -> 77 passed in 3.11s, proving fastmcp is installed in CI (not skipped). AC4 every job pins 'uv venv --python 3.12' (ci.yml 19/29/38/55/64) -> satisfies requires-python>=3.12. AC5 briefs job writes PM/QA/DevOps briefs to $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY (ci.yml 66-91). AC6 pytest 77 passed locally. URL: https://github.com/kevglynn/cursor-takehome-assignment/actions/runs/28242226261 . Non-blocking: Node20 deprecation warning for checkout@v4/setup-uv@v6 (cosmetic).

title: Interview-hardening: make every demo claim verifiably true
description:
WHAT: A hardening/verification phase that closes the gap between what the artifact CLAIMS (in docs/demo.md, README, and the deck) and what is actually wired up and proven. The build phase (epic pbq) produced a complete skeleton with 61 passing local tests, but an honest audit found three claims that are not yet true and one correctness bug.

WHY: This artifact is the centerpiece of a live 45-minute Solutions Architect technical screen. Any claim that fails under interviewer scrutiny (e.g. "here is a green CI run" when Actions is not running, or "the MCP server feeds Cursor" when no .cursor/mcp.json exists) converts a strength into a credibility hit. The goal is zero gap between narration and reality.

WHERE: .github/workflows/ci.yml, pyproject.toml, GitHub repo Actions settings, .cursor/mcp.json (new), .cursor/rules/*.mdc, src/ob/mcp_server.py, src/ob/guardrails.py, docs/demo.md, README.md.

AUDIT FINDINGS being addressed:
1. GitHub Actions endpoints return 404 (Actions not running) AND the test job installs .[dev] while fastmcp lives in .[mcp], so MCP tests would fail to import -> CI is both absent and would be red. demo.md line 242 says "show a green CI run on GitHub."
2. No .cursor/mcp.json exists, so the MCP server is code-only and never loaded by Cursor. demo.md lines 343-345 claim the MCP server feeds Cursor's context engine.
3. The "strong yes" centerpiece (a Cursor rule catching a violation live in the editor, demo.md Section 1) is unverified in the Cursor runtime.
4. ob scaffold --task "add adaptive histogram equalization helper" lands files in skimage/io/ because "io" is a substring of "equalizatION" — naive substring matching in guardrails.extract_target_directory.

Non-goals: no re-architecture; no new product features beyond the MCP tool elevation (child .5); not changing the deterministic-CLI decision.
acceptance_criteria:
SUCCESS CRITERIA (epic closes when all children close with evidence):
- Every command and claim in docs/demo.md is reproducible from a fresh clone, or has a documented, rehearsed fallback.
- CI runs on GitHub and concludes success on main, including MCP tests.
- The MCP server is registered via .cursor/mcp.json and its resources are loadable.
- The live Cursor-rule "catch" moment has a written, rehearsed verification protocol plus a deterministic fallback.
- ob scaffold routes tasks to the correct approved directory with no false substring matches.
- No documented claim in README/demo.md/deck is contradicted by actual behavior.

title: Demo walkthrough script + pre-baked examples
description:
THE SPEC THAT DRIVES EVERYTHING ELSE.

Write the demo walkthrough script (docs/demo.md) as a minute-by-minute specification for the 45-minute live interview. This is bead 0 because the council unanimously agreed: design the demo first, build backward from it. Every other bead exists to serve a beat in this script.

Also create the two committed example workspaces that the demo depends on:
- examples/bad-first-contrib/ — a workspace with intentional convention violations (deprecated API usage, missing test file, TODO-only implementation, import from private module)
- examples/safe-first-contrib/ — a clean workspace that passes all checks

Files:
- docs/demo.md (the walkthrough script with timing, narration cues, and fallback plans)
- examples/bad-first-contrib/ (source file, test file with violations, PLAN.md)
- examples/safe-first-contrib/ (source file, proper test file, PLAN.md, BRIEFS.md)
- docs/known-limitations.md (pre-written honest limitations list)

The demo script structure (council-converged):
0:00-0:05 — Hook: frame the pain, show bad-first-contrib in Cursor, agent catches violation via rule
0:05-0:10 — Same check from terminal: ob check examples/bad-first-contrib → identical failures
0:10-0:16 — Show the workflow: scaffold → check passes on clean example
0:16-0:23 — Multi-audience: 4 role briefs, point at differences
0:23-0:28 — Live mutation: edit profiles/scikit-image.yaml in Cursor, rerun check, new failure appears

0:33-0:38 — Tradeoffs, limitations, round-4 extensibility

Non-goals:
- Not implementing the CLI or Cursor rules yet (those are separate beads)
- Not writing production code; this is the script/spec the other beads build toward

Approach:
1. Write docs/demo.md with the full walkthrough script, including narration text, commands to run, expected outputs, and fallback plans for each section
2. Create the bad-first-contrib example with realistic scikit-image-style code containing seeded violations

acceptance_criteria:

- Each section specifies: timing, narration cues, commands to run, expected output description, and fallback plan if something goes wrong

- examples/safe-first-contrib/ contains clean code that follows all scikit-image conventions from the profile

- The walkthrough can be narrated end-to-end in under 45 minutes by reading the script
- The quotable line appears in the first section: "Most candidates build a coding assistant for one engineer. I built a convention-aware SDLC workflow that turns tribal knowledge into an executable artifact."

title: Engineering Onboarding Copilot
description:
Build a Cursor-native Engineering Onboarding Copilot that guides new engineers through safe first contributions to scikit-image. The artifact is a two-layer system: Cursor workspace configuration (rules, MCP, docs context) as the primary IDE surface, and a deterministic Python CLI (`ob`) as the CI/DevOps engine.

The copilot must demonstrate the full SDLC path (plan, design, build, review, test, CI/deploy) and serve four audiences: engineer, PM, QA, DevOps. It will be presented in a 45-minute live walkthrough for a Cursor Solutions Architect technical interview.

Key architectural decisions (council-validated):
- Cursor is the protagonist; the CLI is the engine
- 100% deterministic CLI; LLM interaction happens only through Cursor's own agent
- Profile-driven design: conventions live in YAML, owned by the team, extensible by swapping profiles
- FastMCP server exposes conventions as resources to Cursor's context engine
- Demo script is the spec; build backward from the walkthrough

Non-goals:
- Not a chatbot or general-purpose coding assistant
- Not an LLM wrapper; no model API calls in the CLI
- Not a deep scikit-image encyclopedia; profile is minimal but realistic
- Not a production-grade tool; it's a demo artifact that shows SA judgment
acceptance_criteria:
- Cloning the repo and opening in Cursor produces a guided onboarding experience via rules
- `ob scaffold`, `ob check`, and `ob brief` commands work end-to-end
- Cursor rules catch convention violations in the editor in real time
- MCP server exposes scikit-image conventions as resources to Cursor
- Multi-role briefs (engineer, PM, QA, DevOps) produce meaningfully differentiated output
- CI pipeline runs the same checks the engineer runs locally
- Live 45-minute walkthrough can be executed without external dependencies
- A second library can be supported by adding a new profile YAML (extensibility proven)
close_reason: All child beads (pbq.1 through pbq.7) closed successfully. Full Engineering Onboarding Copilot artifact complete and pushed to main.

title: Reposition demo narrative: prevention-first framing, linter differentiation, platform proof
description:

The framing should be internalized for live delivery, not over-encoded into rewritten slides. One line in Section 1 is enough to make it explicit. The rest is confidence in what is already built.

title: Post-analysis repositioning: demo-to-deployable narrative and content hardening
description:
WHAT: A repositioning pass based on a four-perspective adversarial analysis (Customer VP Eng, SA Hiring Panel, Implementation Skeptic, Cursor Sales/CS). All four voices converged on: (1) the architecture is sound, (2) the demo lands, (3) the brief content is the weakest link, (4) the prevention-not-detection framing is underplayed, (5) the linter-with-extra-steps objection needs a pre-emptive answer. This epic addresses the highest-impact findings without over-engineering.

WHERE: docs/demo.md, docs/deck.md, docs/known-limitations.md, new docs/deployment-guide.md, src/ob/commands/brief.py, src/ob/commands/check.py, src/ob/commands/scaffold.py, tests/.

These changes move it from opens-the-conversation toward closes-the-deal by pre-empting the strongest objections, filling the content gaps the panel identified, and making production-credibility signals visible.

Non-goals: no architectural rewrites, no LLM in the CLI, no multi-profile composition implementation (acknowledged only), no new check types, no full diffusers profile.
acceptance_criteria:
- Demo narrative explicitly articulates the creation-time prevention value (MCP context injection) distinct from commit-time detection (linter).
- The strongest customer objection (linter with extra steps) has a prepared, rehearsed counter-argument in the Q&A.
- A deployment guide exists showing the rollout path for a real org.
- The engineer brief demonstrates code-awareness (AST-derived observations, not just metadata).
- Production-credibility signals are visible: inline suppression, informational scaffold findings.

title: Generate Cursor convention rule from profile + CI drift check
description: WHAT: .cursor/rules/scikit-image-conventions.mdc and the sdlc rules are hand-written copies of the profile that falsely claim to be loaded from or generated from the YAML and drift on any profile edit or swap. WHERE: scripts/render_rules.py (new), .cursor/rules/*.mdc, .github/workflows/ci.yml, README and docs. HOW: write a generator that renders the convention rule body from the active profile (approved dirs, forbidden paths, deprecated APIs, rule IDs, docstring and testing conventions) with a GENERATED edit-the-profile-not-this-file banner; regenerate the checked-in rule; add a CI step that regenerates and fails if the working tree differs; remove or repair the false provenance wording. Non-goals: not generating the generic SDLC rule prose, only the convention content. Flagged by architect C1. Depends on profile validation.
acceptance_criteria: Running the generator reproduces the checked-in convention rule byte for byte. Editing the profile then regenerating changes the rule. CI fails if the profile changed without regeneration. No rule file claims to be auto-loaded from the YAML unless it is actually generated.
close_reason: Cursor convention rule now generated from profile via src/ob/render_rules.py + scripts/render_rules.py wrapper. title: Bookend slide deck (Marp markdown + PDF backup)
description:
Create a bookend slide deck that frames the live demo walkthrough: 3 slides to open (set context, land the pain, show the architecture), 3 slides to close (tradeoffs, extensibility, and the ask/close). The deck lives in the repo as a single Marp markdown file, version-controlled alongside the artifact it presents.

WHY THIS MATTERS:
An SA walkthrough without visual framing forces the audience to build context from narration alone during the opening minutes — the highest-stakes window for audience engagement. The bookend structure solves this: slides control the room for the first 3 minutes (before any terminal is visible), then the live demo takes over (where the artifact earns its keep), then slides return to frame tradeoffs and extensibility (where strategic thinking matters more than running code). The deck never competes with the live demo; it frames it.

WHY MARP:
The deck format itself sends a signal. At a Cursor interview, opening a browser-based presentation tool (Google Slides, Figma) mid-demo breaks the spell and signals the candidate doesn't live in the editor. A Marp markdown file in the repo says "I treat everything as code" — it's version-controlled, agent-updatable, and renderable from the terminal. The PDF export is a cold backup for projector/renderer failures.

WHERE IN THE CODEBASE:
- docs/deck.md (Marp markdown source)
- docs/deck.pdf (pre-rendered PDF backup, committed)
- docs/assets/ (any images: architecture diagram, pain-point visual)

SLIDE STRUCTURE (6 slides max):

Opening bookend (shown before any terminal/Cursor is visible):
1. TITLE + HOOK — "Engineering Onboarding Copilot" + the one-line pain statement: "New engineers lose days discovering conventions. Reviewers lose hours correcting avoidable issues. PM/QA/DevOps lack visibility into how a change moves safely toward production."
2. ARCHITECTURE — Two-layer diagram: Cursor workspace (rules + MCP) as the IDE surface, CLI (ob) as the CI/DevOps engine, profile YAML as the shared source of truth. Shows how the three components connect and who owns what.
3. APPROACH — What I built, what I deliberately didn't build, and why. The three-column layout: "Built" / "Deferred" / "Why." This pre-empts the tradeoff question and demonstrates scope judgment before the demo even starts.

Closing bookend (shown after the live demo):
4. TRADEOFFS + LIMITATIONS — The honest list: deterministic by design (no LLM in CLI), single-profile scope, template-driven briefs, hand-curated conventions. Each with "how I'd address it" in a real engagement.
5. EXTENSIBILITY + ROUND 4 — The profile-driven extensibility story: "Swap the YAML, keep the engine." Shows the diffusers stub profile as evidence. Explicitly addresses "how would a different team extend this?"
6. CLOSE — The quotable line. "Most candidates build a coding assistant for one engineer. I built a convention-aware SDLC workflow that turns tribal knowledge into an executable artifact — owned by the team, usable by PM/QA/DevOps, and extensible to any library by swapping a YAML profile." Plus: "Questions?"

DESIGN CONSTRAINTS:
- Dark theme (projector-friendly, matches terminal aesthetic)
- Minimal text per slide (no walls of text; the narration carries the content)
- Architecture diagram should be clean and readable at projector resolution
- Consistent with the Rich output color palette in the CLI (visual coherence between slides and terminal)
- No animations or transitions (reliable rendering across environments)

Non-goals:
- Not a product deck or marketing material; this is an SA walkthrough companion
- Not more than 6 slides; anything beyond that steals time from the live demo
- Not a standalone presentation; slides are meaningless without the live demo they frame
- Not Figma, not Google Slides, not Keynote — Marp markdown only

Approach:
1. Install Marp CLI as a dev dependency (npx @marp-team/marp-cli)
2. Write docs/deck.md with Marp frontmatter (theme, size, paginate)
3. Create or source a clean architecture diagram for slide 2
4. Render to HTML for dev preview: npx @marp-team/marp-cli docs/deck.md --html
5. Render to PDF for backup: npx @marp-team/marp-cli docs/deck.md --pdf
6. Commit both deck.md and deck.pdf
7. Verify rendering on a secondary display or at projector resolution (1920x1080)

Testing approach (feature bead — ACs become tests):
- No unit tests (this is a presentation artifact)
- Verification: Marp renders deck.md without errors
- Verification: PDF export produces a readable 6-page document
- Verification: architecture diagram is legible at 1920x1080
- Verification: text is readable from 3 meters (projector distance)
acceptance_criteria:
- docs/deck.md exists as a valid Marp markdown file with frontmatter (marp: true, theme, size)
- Deck contains exactly 6 slides: title+hook, architecture, approach, tradeoffs, extensibility, close
- Slide 1 contains the pain statement and project name
- Slide 2 contains a two-layer architecture diagram showing Cursor rules + MCP / CLI / Profile YAML and their relationships
- Slide 3 shows a "Built / Deferred / Why" layout demonstrating scope judgment
- Slide 4 lists at least 5 specific limitations with "how I'd address it" notes
- Slide 5 references the diffusers stub profile as extensibility evidence
- Slide 6 contains the quotable line verbatim
- npx @marp-team/marp-cli docs/deck.md --html renders without errors

- Dark theme applied (projector-friendly)

- Architecture diagram is readable at 1920x1080 resolution

title: CLI engine: scaffold + check + brief commands
description:
Implement the three core CLI commands that form the deterministic engine of the copilot. These are 100% deterministic — no LLM calls. This is a deliberate design decision: pushing more LLM calls out of sight isn't the right call when agentic development is already the focal point. Better to have transparency about what's happening LLM-/token-wise. The CLI is the repeatable, inspectable, team-ownable layer.

Commands:

1. ob scaffold --task "..." [--profile PATH]
   Creates a new workspace under workspaces/<slug>/ with:
   - PLAN.md (contribution plan citing profile rule IDs — looks like convention reasoning, not template stamping)
   - Source file stub in the correct directory per profile
   - Test file stub following the profile's test conventions
   - BRIEFS.md with all four role sections (engineer, PM, QA, DevOps)
   Guardrails: refuses to write outside approved directories defined in the profile.

2. ob check <workspace> [--profile PATH]
   Validates a workspace against the profile. Catches:
   - Missing test files
   - TODO-only implementation (no real logic)
   - Imports from forbidden/private modules
   - Deprecated API usage (from profile's deprecated_apis list)
   - Files outside approved directories
   Outputs: Rich-formatted pass/fail summary with rule IDs and explanations.
   Must produce identical output whether run locally or in CI.

3. ob brief --role engineer|pm|qa|devops [--workspace PATH] [--profile PATH]
   Generates role-specific summaries from the workspace metadata:
   - Engineer: implementation checklist + file map + convention reminders
   - PM: user/business impact + scope + "what to ask in kickoff"
   - QA: edge cases + test strategy + "test names we will reject"
   - DevOps: CI guardrails exercised + pipeline signal + boundary enforcement status
   Output is Rich-formatted for terminal AND raw markdown for CI piping.

Files:
- src/ob/commands/scaffold.py
- src/ob/commands/check.py
- src/ob/commands/brief.py

title: Foundation: project scaffold + scikit-image profile
description:
Create the Python project foundation and the scikit-image conventions profile that powers the entire system.

The project scaffold is the standard Python CLI skeleton: pyproject.toml, src layout, tests, ruff config, pytest config, and the ob CLI entry point via Typer. The CLI must print help and version on day one.

The scikit-image profile (profiles/scikit-image.yaml) is the architectural ace of this project. It encodes library conventions as structured data that the CLI reads, the Cursor rules reference, and the MCP server exposes. The profile is team-owned — a platform team can update conventions without touching code. This design is what makes the system extensible to any library by swapping a YAML file.

Also create a stub profile for HuggingFace diffusers (profiles/diffusers.yaml) with enough rules to prove extensibility in the walkthrough. This is NOT a fully-fleshed profile — just enough to answer "how would a different team extend this?"

Files:
- pyproject.toml (Python 3.12+, Typer, Rich, Pydantic, PyYAML, pytest, ruff)
- src/ob/__init__.py, src/ob/cli.py, src/ob/profile.py, src/ob/models.py
- profiles/scikit-image.yaml
- profiles/diffusers.yaml (stub)
- tests/test_profile.py
- tests/conftest.py

Profile schema (Pydantic-validated):
- name, version, description
- approved_directories (where new code can go)
- test_conventions (naming, location, required patterns)
- forbidden_paths (private modules, internal APIs)
- deprecated_apis (list with replacement suggestions)
- contribution_checklist (per-role items)
- docstring_convention (numpydoc, google, etc.)
- role_brief_templates (per-role template sections)

Non-goals:
- Not implementing scaffold/check/brief commands yet (separate bead)
- Not exhaustive scikit-image coverage; profile should be realistic but minimal
- Diffusers profile is a stub, not production-quality

Approach:
1. Create pyproject.toml with all deps, build config, and tool configs (ruff, pytest)
2. Create src/ob/cli.py with Typer app and placeholder commands (--help works)
3. Create src/ob/profile.py with Pydantic models for the profile schema
4. Create profiles/scikit-image.yaml with real conventions (use battle cards as reference)
5. Create profiles/diffusers.yaml stub with 3-4 rules
6. Write tests for profile loading, validation, and schema enforcement
7. Verify: ob --help works, pytest passes, ruff passes

Testing approach (pragmatic TDD):
- Feature bead → ACs become tests
- Test profile loading from YAML
- Test Pydantic validation catches malformed profiles
- Test that profile fields are accessible and correctly typed
- Test that both profiles load without error

- pyproject.toml defines the project with all dependencies and tool configs
- ob --help prints available commands (scaffold, check, brief) even if they're stubs

- Profile schema enforces required fields: name, approved_directories, test_conventions, forbidden_paths, deprecated_apis

- pytest passes with at least 5 tests covering profile loading and validation
- ruff check passes with zero errors

title: Fix scaffold target-directory inference (word-boundary + keyword map)
description:
WHAT: guardrails.extract_target_directory matches an approved module when its name appears as a SUBSTRING of the task text. This produces false matches: `ob scaffold --task "add adaptive histogram equalization helper"` routes to skimage/io/ because "io" is a substring of "equalizatION". The correct destination is skimage/exposure/ (histogram equalization is an exposure operation). Naive substring matching undercuts the "the tool knows the conventions" narrative if it surfaces live.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. From the repo root (venv active): `ob scaffold --task "add adaptive histogram equalization helper"`.
2. Inspect the created tree: files land under `workspaces/.../skimage/io/` instead of `skimage/exposure/`.
3. Root cause: in guardrails.extract_target_directory, `"io" in "add adaptive histogram equalization helper".lower()` is True because "io" is a substring of "equalizatION".

WHERE: src/ob/guardrails.py (extract_target_directory), src/ob/commands/scaffold.py (fallback default), tests/test_scaffold.py; optionally profiles/scikit-image.yaml if keyword hints are encoded data-side.

HOW:
- Replace substring matching with word-boundary/token matching so "io" no longer matches inside "equalization".
- Add a deterministic keyword->module synonym map covering common scikit-image tasks (e.g. histogram/contrast/equaliz/exposure -> exposure; edge/threshold/gaussian/blur/filter -> filters; denoise/deconvolv/restore -> restoration; segment/watershed/slic -> segmentation; morpholog/erode/dilate -> morphology; transform/rotate/resize/warp -> transform; color/rgb/gray -> color). Prefer encoding hints in the profile YAML to keep it data-driven and team-owned, consistent with the architecture.
- Choose a sensible fallback default (e.g. skimage/filters/) when nothing matches, instead of approved_directories[0].
- Cover the mappings with tests, including a regression test for the equalization/io false match.

WHY: Scaffold may be shown live (demo.md Section 2). Correct routing reinforces the core thesis; a nonsensical directory undermines it. Keeping hints in the profile also reinforces the "team owns the YAML" story.

Non-goals: no ML/NLP classifier; deterministic keyword mapping only; not expanding the approved-directory list.
acceptance_criteria:
- `ob scaffold --task "add adaptive histogram equalization helper"` creates files under skimage/exposure/ (not skimage/io/).
- "edge detection filter" routes to skimage/filters/; "image denoising" routes to skimage/restoration/; "watershed segmentation" routes to skimage/segmentation/.
- The token "io" inside "equalization" no longer triggers a match on the io module (explicit regression test).
- A sensible default directory is used when no keyword matches (documented in code).
- tests/test_scaffold.py covers >= 5 task->directory mappings plus the false-match regression; all scaffold tests pass.
- ruff check and ruff format remain clean; full pytest suite stays green.
close_reason: Commit 9753f4c on main. AC1 (histogram equalization -> skimage/exposure/): test_keyword_routing + test_scaffold_routes_equalization_to_exposure pass. AC2 (edge->filters, denoising->restoration, watershed->segmentation): parametrized test_keyword_routing passes for all. AC3 ('io' no longer matches inside 'equalization'): test_io_not_matched_inside_equalization passes; word-boundary regex \\b prevents substring hits. AC4 (sensible default when no match): default_directory=skimage/filters/ in profile, used by scaffold; test_unmatched_task_returns_none confirms None->caller default. AC5 (>=5 mappings + regression covered): 6 parametrized + regression + whole-word + e2e. AC6 (ruff clean, suite green): 75 tests pass, ruff check + format clean.

title: CI pipeline + role-brief delivery
description:
Create the GitHub Actions CI pipeline that proves the same checks an engineer runs locally also run in the production path. This closes the SDLC loop: authoring-time safety (Cursor rules) → review-time safety (ob check) → production-time safety (CI).

The CI pipeline also delivers multi-role briefs via \$GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY, making the multi-audience claim observable rather than asserted. A PM looking at the PR sees the PM brief in the Actions summary tab without cloning a repo or running a CLI.

Files:
- .github/workflows/ci.yml
- (updates to ob brief to support --format markdown output for CI piping)

Pipeline steps:
1. Checkout + Python setup
2. Install dependencies (pip install -e ".[dev]")
3. ruff check . (linting)
4. ruff format --check . (formatting)
5. pytest (tests)
6. ob check examples/bad-first-contrib (expected to find violations — verify exit code)
7. ob check examples/safe-first-contrib (expected to pass clean)
8. ob brief --role pm --format markdown >> \$GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
9. ob brief --role qa --format markdown >> \$GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
10. ob brief --role devops --format markdown >> \$GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY

Non-goals:
- No deployment step (this is a take-home, not a production service)
- No secrets required
- No Docker or complex infrastructure
- No coverage thresholds (pragmatic TDD, not coverage theater)

Approach:
1. Create .github/workflows/ci.yml with the pipeline steps
2. Ensure ob brief --format markdown produces clean markdown suitable for GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
3. Test locally with act or by running the same commands manually
4. Push and verify the pipeline runs green on GitHub

Testing approach (chore bead — no unit tests required):
- Verification is the pipeline running green on GitHub
- Local verification: run the same commands in sequence and confirm exit codes
acceptance_criteria:
- .github/workflows/ci.yml exists and runs on push to main and on pull requests
- Pipeline runs: ruff check, ruff format --check, pytest, ob check on both example workspaces, ob brief for PM/QA/DevOps roles
- ob check examples/bad-first-contrib exits non-zero (violations expected)
- ob check examples/safe-first-contrib exits zero (clean pass)
- Role briefs appear in GitHub Actions step summary (GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY)
- Pipeline requires no secrets or external services
- Pipeline runs green on GitHub after push (all steps pass)
- The same ob check command produces identical output locally and in CI

title: Add ob:ignore inline suppression for convention violations
description:
WHAT: Support a comment-based suppression mechanism (# ob:ignore or # ob:ignore SK-D-001) that lets engineers skip specific violations on specific lines when they have a legitimate reason.

WHERE: src/ob/commands/check.py (all check functions), tests/test_check.py.

HOW:
- Before reporting a violation, check if the relevant source line (or the line above it) contains # ob:ignore (suppress all) or # ob:ignore RULE-ID (suppress specific rule).
- If suppressed, skip that violation in the result.
- Document the mechanism in a short section in README.

WHY: The Implementation Skeptic flagged that production engineers will hit legitimate exceptions. No escape hatch means the tool becomes noise that engineers learn to ignore entirely. This is a small code change that hugely improves production-credibility -- it shows the SA has thought about real-world usage, not just demo scenarios.

Non-goals: not adding a .obignore file (keep it inline for now); not adding severity levels; not suppressing at the file or directory level.
acceptance_criteria:
- A source line annotated with # ob:ignore suppresses all violations reported against that line.
- A source line annotated with # ob:ignore SK-D-001 suppresses only that specific rule, while other violations on the same line still fire.
- Suppression comment on the line above a violating line also works (for cases where the violation line itself cannot be commented).

title: Scaffold emits completion-status markers visible to ob check
description:
WHAT: When ob scaffold creates stub files, have ob check report scaffold-incomplete: N functions still have NotImplementedError as an informational finding -- not a blocking violation, but a visible signal that the scaffold output is a starting point, not a finished contribution.

WHERE: src/ob/commands/scaffold.py (emit marker), src/ob/commands/check.py (detect and report), tests/test_scaffold.py, tests/test_check.py.

HOW:
- Scaffold: use the existing NotImplementedError raise as the detection signal (no new markers needed).
- Check: add an informational-severity finding (not an error) when NotImplementedError or pytest.skip("Not yet implemented") is detected. Report as INFO, not as a rule violation. Does not affect exit code.
- This closes the gap where scaffold output passes ob check silently despite being hollow.

WHY: The Skeptic identified the scaffold paradox -- output passes ob check but contributes nothing. An informational signal bridges the gap between the-tool-blocks-bad-code and the-tool-guides-toward-complete-code without being punitive toward work-in-progress.

Non-goals: not blocking CI on scaffold-incomplete findings; not changing scaffold output structure; purely informational.
acceptance_criteria:
- ob check on a freshly-scaffolded workspace reports INFO-level findings for NotImplementedError stubs (visible in output, does not cause non-zero exit).
- ob check on examples/safe-first-contrib (no stubs) reports zero INFO findings.
- The INFO findings do not appear in the violation count narrated in the demo (locked test still passes with 5 violations for bad-first-contrib).
